title: The Principles of Mean Reversion
author: Jose C S
keyHighlights: Mean reversion is based on the statistical tendency of prices to return to their historical average.
Indicators like Bollinger Bands and RSI help identify extreme overbought or oversold conditions.
Risk management is critical since assets can stay stretched or enter structural trend changes.

content.text: Mean reversion is one of the oldest and most reliable concepts in financial trading. It operates on the simple premise that price fluctuations are temporary and will eventually gravitate back to a long-term average or mean value.

To capture mean reversion opportunities, traders measure the distance of current price from a moving average using standard deviations. When price moves two or three standard deviations away from its 20-period simple moving average, it is statistically considered extended, indicating a high probability of a retracement.

content.caption: Bollinger Bands plot standard deviation bands around a central moving average, highlighting stretch points.

content.text: The greatest trap for mean reversion traders is the breakout. If an asset undergoes a structural shift, such as a major technological breakthrough or regulatory ban, the price will not revert; instead, it will establish a new trend. Therefore, tight stop losses and validation from volume profiles are necessary before entering reversion trades.
By combining statistical indicators with market breadth analysis, swing traders can capture high-probability counter-trend setups with clear, defined risk parameters.
